title: Python's secret hooks
instruction:
Have you ever wondered how `print(myList)` knows to show `[1, 2, 3]`? Or how `len(myList)` knows the count?

Python has hooks — special methods with double underscores — that you can implement on your own classes to make them behave like built-ins.

`print(obj)` calls `obj.__str__()`.
`len(obj)` calls `obj.__len__()`.
`obj1 + obj2` calls `obj1.__add__(obj2)`.

Run this. Notice print(book) shows the memory address — useless. Your job is to fix that.
starter:
class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, author, pages):
        self.title = title
        self.author = author
        self.pages = pages

book = Book("Python Crash Course", "Eric Matthes", 544)
print(book)  # right now: <__main__.Book object at 0x...>
successMessage: Gibberish output. Now you know WHY — no __str__ defined. Let's fix that next.

title: __str__ vs __repr__
instruction:
Two methods, two purposes:

- `__str__` — human-friendly output for `print()`
- `__repr__` — developer-friendly, for debugging. Convention: should look like Python code that recreates the object

Add both to `Book`.
starter:
class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, author, pages):
        self.title = title
        self.author = author
        self.pages = pages
    
    # TODO: add __str__ and __repr__

book = Book("Python Crash Course", "Eric Matthes", 544)
print(book)        # uses __str__
print(repr(book))  # uses __repr__
expectedOutput: Python Crash Course by Eric Matthes

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: __str__ for humans, __repr__ for developers. If you only implement one, implement __repr__ — Python falls back to it for __str__ if __str__ isn't defined.

title: __len__ — make len() work
instruction:
`len(obj)` calls `obj.__len__()`. Add it to your class and len() just works.

Add `__len__` to `Playlist` so `len(playlist)` returns the song count.
starter:
class Playlist:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.songs = []
    
    def add_song(self, song):
        self.songs.append(song)
    
    # TODO: add __len__ so len(playlist) works

playlist = Playlist("Favourites")
playlist.add_song("Song A")
playlist.add_song("Song B")
playlist.add_song("Song C")
print(len(playlist))  # 3

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: len(playlist) works just like len([1,2,3]). Your Playlist now behaves like a Python built-in. That's the magic of dunder methods.

title: __eq__ — make == work properly
instruction:
By default, `==` on objects checks memory address — two different objects are never equal, even with identical data.

Implement `__eq__` to compare by *value* instead:

```python
def __eq__(self, other):
    return self.x == other.x and self.y == other.y
```

Add `__eq__` to `Point` so two points with the same coordinates are equal.
hint:
def __eq__(self, other):
    return self.x == other.x and self.y == other.y
starter:
class Point:
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x = x
        self.y = y
    
    # TODO: add __eq__ to compare by x and y values

p1 = Point(3, 4)
p2 = Point(3, 4)
p3 = Point(1, 2)

print(p1 == p2)  # should be True
print(p1 == p3)  # should be False

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: p1 == p2 is now True! Two different objects but same values — equal. Without __eq__ they would never be equal (different memory addresses). Value equality vs identity.

title: __lt__ — make sorting work
instruction:
Want to use `sorted()` on a list of your custom objects? You need `__lt__` (less than).

Add `__lt__` to `Student` so you can sort by score.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Sorted by score! Once __lt__ is defined, sorted() knows how to order your students. You can also use @functools.total_ordering to generate all comparison methods from just __eq__ and __lt__.

title: __add__ — overload the + operator
instruction:
`v1 + v2` calls `v1.__add__(v2)`. Implement it and the + operator works on your class.

Add `__add__` to `Vector` so you can add two vectors.
hint:
def __add__(self, other):
    return Vector(self.x + other.x, self.y + other.y)

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: v1 + v2 gives Vector(4, 6) — a brand new vector. Operator overloading makes your class feel native. You can also add __sub__, __mul__, __truediv__ for full math support.

title: __contains__ — make 'in' work
instruction:
`x in obj` calls `obj.__contains__(x)`. Add it to your class and the `in` keyword just works.

Add `__contains__` to `Playlist` so `"Song" in playlist` works.
hint:
def __contains__(self, song):
    return song in self.songs
starter:
class Playlist:
    def __init__(self):
        self.songs = []
    
    def add(self, song):
        self.songs.append(song)
    
    # TODO: add __contains__ so 'song in playlist' works

playlist = Playlist()
playlist.add("Bohemian Rhapsody")
playlist.add("Hotel California")

print("Hotel California" in playlist)    # True
print("Stairway to Heaven" in playlist)  # False

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: 'Hotel California' in playlist just works! __contains__ is what makes custom collections feel native. Your class now supports Python's most readable way to check membership.

title: Build a Money class
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a `Money` class:
- `__init__(amount, currency)` — store both
- `__str__` — returns e.g. `"$10.00"`
- `__repr__` — returns e.g. `"Money(10.0, 'USD')"`
- `__add__` — add two Money objects of the same currency
- `__eq__` — equal if same amount AND same currency

Test all four operations.
successMessage: A Money class that feels like a Python built-in. You can print it, add it, compare it. Dunder methods transform plain classes into professional types. Chapter 16 done!
